title: Cherry clafoutis
subtitle: Baked French batter pudding with whole cherries
excerpt: Cherry clafoutis is a baked Limousin pudding: whole cherries arranged in a buttered dish, covered with a light batter of eggs, milk, cream and flour, and baked until puffed and lightly set. It is made in 15 minutes of active work. The batter sinks back as it cools; this is normal. It is served warm, dusted with icing sugar.
sourceNotes: Clafoutis originates in the Limousin region of central France and is documented in French culinary texts from the nineteenth century. The traditional recipe uses whole, unpitted cherries: the stones release a faint almond flavour during baking. Modern versions commonly pit the cherries for easier eating. Outside cherry season, the same batter works with other stone fruit (plums, apricots) under the name flaugnarde. This recipe is synthesised from the established Limousin clafoutis tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best served within an hour of baking. Can be made earlier in the day and warmed briefly before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional Limousin clafoutis is made with whole unpitted cherries. The stones release a faint almond flavour into the batter during baking that is considered part of the dish. The practical argument against leaving stones in is obvious: diners must eat carefully. For home baking where guests may not expect whole stones, pit the cherries. If you leave them in, warn your guests. Either way the clafoutis bakes and tastes the same.

content.text: is yours. The batter is the same either way: it should flow freely and coat the cherries as it sets. Do not overmix after adding the flour or the clafoutis will be tough. Rest the batter for 10 minutes before pouring to let the flour settle.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Butter a 23 cm pie dish or gratin dish generously. Scatter the 500 g cherries over the base in a single layer. Whisk the 3 eggs and 100 g caster sugar together in a bowl until combined. Sift in the 75 g flour and whisk until smooth. Add the 250 ml milk, 100 ml cream and vanilla extract and whisk until the batter is lump-free. Rest for 10 minutes, then pour over the cherries. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the batter is puffed and set at the edges, with a slight wobble in the centre, and the top is lightly coloured. It will sink as it cools. Dust generously with icing sugar and serve warm.

items.problem: Clafoutis is very dense and heavy
items.solution: The batter was over-mixed or the flour was not sifted, creating pockets of dry flour that absorbed too much liquid. Sift the flour, whisk until just smooth, then stop.
This is expected and normal in a clafoutis. Arrange the cherries in a single layer rather than piling them up. The batter sets around them as it bakes.
